content.content:
1	//! MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that exposes an Orbit tool surface to
2	//! any MCP-capable client.
3	//!
4	//! The crate is a thin transport adapter between rmcp's server runtime and an
5	//! Orbit-supplied [`McpHost`]. `orbit-mcp` itself performs no tool dispatch,
6	//! no policy evaluation, and no audit logging — it delegates each
7	//! `tools/call` to the host, which in the default `orbit-cli` wiring routes
8	//! through `OrbitRuntime::execute_tool_command` and therefore honors the same
9	//! policy chain, disabled-tool flag, and audit events as the CLI path.
10	//!
11	//! # Role
12	//! Depends on `orbit-types` and `orbit-tools` only (for [`orbit_common::types::ToolSchema`]
13	//! and MCP-shape helpers). The CLI constructs a runtime-backed [`McpHost`] and
14	//! hands it to [`serve_stdio`]. No dependency on `orbit-core` is introduced.
15	//!
16	//! # Transport
17	//! Only stdio is supported in this cut. HTTP/SSE/streamable-http transports
18	//! are follow-up work once authentication is in scope.
19	
20	mod adapter;
21	mod error;
22	
23	use std::sync::Arc;
24	
25	use orbit_common::types::{OrbitError, ToolSchema};
26	use rmcp::ServiceExt;
27	use rmcp::transport::io::stdio;
28	use serde_json::Value;
29	
30	pub use adapter::OrbitToolServer;
31	
32	/// A pluggable back-end that satisfies MCP `tools/list` and `tools/call`
33	/// requests.
34	///
35	/// `list_tool_schemas` is expected to return only the tools the host wants
36	/// exposed — disabled tools should be filtered out here, not in the adapter.
37	/// `call_tool` must itself run whatever policy, audit, and sandboxing the host
38	/// wants applied; the adapter will never bypass it.
39	pub trait McpHost: Send + Sync + 'static {
40	    fn list_tool_schemas(&self) -> Vec<ToolSchema>;
41	    fn call_tool(&self, name: &str, input: Value) -> Result<Value, OrbitError>;
42	}
43	
44	/// Serve the given [`McpHost`] over an MCP stdio transport.
45	///
46	/// Runs until the client disconnects or the server encounters a fatal
47	/// transport error. The function is async and expects to be driven by a tokio
48	/// runtime (see `tokio::runtime::Runtime::block_on`).
49	pub async fn serve_stdio(host: Arc<dyn McpHost>) -> Result<(), OrbitError> {
50	    let server = OrbitToolServer::new(host);
51	    let running = server
52	        .serve(stdio())
53	        .await
54	        .map_err(|err| OrbitError::Execution(format!("mcp serve_stdio start: {err}")))?;
55	    running
56	        .waiting()
57	        .await
58	        .map_err(|err| OrbitError::Execution(format!("mcp serve_stdio wait: {err}")))?;
59	    Ok(())
60	}
61

content.text: Let me also check the error module for completeness.

content.content:
1	use orbit_common::types::OrbitError;
2	use rmcp::model::CallToolResult;
3	use serde_json::{Value, json};
4	
5	/// Map an [`OrbitError`] from tool execution into an `isError: true` MCP
6	/// [`CallToolResult`] with a structured payload.
7	///
8	/// The payload always carries:
9	/// - `code`: a short, stable machine-readable classifier (e.g. `"not_found"`,
10	///   `"invalid_input"`). Callers match on this rather than the free-form text.
11	/// - `message`: the human-readable error message (the `Display` of the error).
12	pub(crate) fn tool_error_result(err: &OrbitError) -> CallToolResult {
13	    CallToolResult::structured_error(error_payload(err))
14	}
15	
16	fn error_payload(err: &OrbitError) -> Value {
17	    json!({
18	        "code": error_code(err),
19	        "message": err.to_string(),
20	    })
21	}
22	
23	fn error_code(err: &OrbitError) -> &'static str {
24	    match err {
25	        OrbitError::ToolNotFound(_) => "tool_not_found",
26	        OrbitError::TaskNotFound(_)
27	        | OrbitError::SkillNotFound(_)
28	        | OrbitError::JobNotFound(_)
29	        | OrbitError::JobRunNotFound(_)
30	        | OrbitError::ActivityNotFound(_)
31	        | OrbitError::AgentSessionNotFound(_)
32	        | OrbitError::WorkspaceNotFound(_) => "not_found",
33	        OrbitError::PolicyDenied(_) => "policy_denied",
34	        OrbitError::TaskApprovalRequired(_) => "approval_required",
35	        OrbitError::InvalidInput(_) => "invalid_input",
36	        OrbitError::SkillValidation(_) | OrbitError::JobValidation(_) => "validation_failed",
37	        OrbitError::TaskStatusTransition(_) | OrbitError::JobRunStateTransition(_) => {
38	            "invalid_transition"
39	        }
40	        OrbitError::AgentProtocolViolation(_) => "agent_protocol_violation",
41	        OrbitError::UnsupportedAgentProvider(_) => "unsupported_provider",
42	        OrbitError::Execution(_) => "execution_failed",
43	        OrbitError::Store(_) => "store_error",
44	        OrbitError::WorkspaceError(_) => "workspace_error",
45	        OrbitError::Io(_) => "io_error",
46	    }
47	}
48

`OrbitToolServer` is public inside the private `adapter` module, but it's re-exported via `pub use adapter::OrbitToolServer`, so it counts as included. For the excluded list, I'm looking at items like the private helper functions in `adapter.rs` (`schema_to_tool`, `build_input_schema`, `property_for`) that aren't re-exported at all.

So my final answer is: included items are `["McpHost", "OrbitToolServer", "serve_stdio"]`, and the only notable excluded item is `tool_error_result` from the private `error` module, which is `pub(crate)` but not publicly visible at the crate root.
